folded on the back of a chair, and the Moleskine with tide charts sits where you left it last night—softly warped at the corners from humidity.
Your fingers find the notebook before your eyes do, tracing
the tide-line sketches, the smudged notes about reed stabilization.
The paper smells faintly of ink and salt.
a small curved turbine vane, filigreed with deliberate dents where he'd welded reinforcements.
It's cool and weighty under your palm.

Mira—For the Saltworks demo.

Call if you want to fit it later.
improvise, test, trust a promise that came in metal and a scribble.
the coral scarf your mother knitted that still smells faintly of lemon soap; the tide-stained bracelet on the
bowl by the sink; the keys with a chipped buoy charm.
Each item is a story, a line in the town's ledger.
Each is small, domestic, stubbornly ordinary.
• Examine the vane closely
You set the tea towel aside and trace the seam where Elias welded the lip.
The metal hums under your fingertip; you imagine it spinning low and steady in the salt-wet wind.
• Wrap it back and make tea
You wrap the vane back in the towel, the weight still a quiet presence.
You light the kettle and let routine steady the morning.
The narrative continues with the choice's consequence integrated into the morning routine.
boil water, press the French press down, breathe until the steam fogs your glasses.
The town moves slowly enough this morning that you can watch
the light change on the counter and feel it ripple through the room—a calm, steady building warmth.
home, home, home.
At the tide-garden plot—your plot—Noah is up on a raised bed, sleeves rolled to forearms, arranging salt-tolerant seedlings under a patched mesh.
Aunt Lila sits on a paint-chipped bench, thermos in hand, knitting quietly.
A patchwork of solar sails flutters above the experimental plots; hoses glisten like small rivers.
There you are.
Comes and goes like the tide, but today you were always meant to be here.
(She smiles, but it's not sharp—more like a fold in fabric that keeps warmth.)
You crouch to check a seed tray.
The soil is cool and damp against your palms.
The Saltworks team—Jun and two others—exchange notes about flow rates; their voices are hushed, careful with numbers and with hope.
We're calibrating the sluice this morning.
If we push a little more volume at low tide, the filtrate beds should take it better.
(He glances at you.) 'You want to look?
We could use a second pair of hands.'
I'll check the distribution valves.
Elias Park left a vane—I've got a prototype to bolt in if the flange holds.
(You pause, trying to make the sentence casual.
It feels like offering a piece of him to the plot, which is both silly and graceful.)
(half-smiles) 'Elias' handiwork?
Well, if it hums like he says, we'll be the loudest reclaimers on this spit.'
(calling down) 'Mira, Aunt Lila says you owe the tide a story.
Or it owes you one.
Either way, better pay up.'
Aunt Lila folds a hand into your hair, then withdraws it—an old ritual of blessing and check.
There is history in that motion; she doesn't need to say it aloud.
You talk about seedlings, about where
torque measurements, whether the bolts are grade-8, the best time to water to avoid salt spray.
There is comfort in the way
the group moves from idea to scaffold—soothing, communal.
• Offer to mount the vane now
You grab a wrench, taste metal and morning on your tongue, and climb the scaffolding to fit the vane.
Your hands remember the snug of bolts and the patient torque of fitting new things into old frames.
• Watch and record notes
You sit on the bench, pen to paper, tracking the team's adjustments.
You love the margin of observation—seeing how small changes add up feels like stitching memory into structure.
The narrative resumes with the selected role in the team's work.
You choose to watch and record—today observation feels right.
You write down the angles Jun calls out, note the way the seedlings turn themselves toward a sliver of sun, jot a line about the vane's welds.
Elias Park messages a picture—a shot of the vane in a vice, his thumb smudged with oil.
A short line If you sense a wobble, don't keep it.
You send a thumbs-up and a heart you consider too intimate for the team chat, but the phone is small and the gesture private.
Conversation meanders back to stories.
Aunt Lila mentions the house by the old estuary—how your mother used to mend nets there, how she tied a small shell to the doorframe to call in luck.
When you
ask if the shell is still there, Aunt Lila's face tightens with that familiar, complex expression—more memory than grief, a living ledger that doesn't demand categorization.
It's complicated.
The shell is there in my head if not on the frame.
Places keep their stories even when boards turn to sea.
It's why we try to make things last, isn't it?
Not always the wood or the stone, but the reasons behind them.
(softly) 'You stitch, Mira.
You always have.'
The Saltworks team tests the sluice.
Water moves with a patient thrum through the mesh—a small, winning sound that nods to labor and craft.
You tuck a strand of hair behind your ear and feel, for a small moment, the gentle satisfaction of work that fits like a well-made seam.
No one mentions it at first—people here tend to let the sea tell its own alarms.
But a woman at a nearby stall holds up her phone with a stunned, polite face.
Her screen shows a
clean, sweeping lines of engineering, a glossy promenade raised and armored, towers in stainless curves that catch light like a promise.
A familiar logo sits in the corner—Whitlock Coastal—Cassandra 'Cass' Whitlock's firm.
Emergency Hearing: Coastal Redevelopment Plan Presented by Whitlock Coastal.
The renderings blink across the screen, each one a vignette of a
future where the old boardwalk is replaced by a levee promenade, neighborhoods homogenized into resilient blocks.
Aunt Lila's knitting rests in her lap like a folded map.
For a heartbeat the air seems to slow; the tide-garden's leaves hold their breath.
(quiet) 'They're moving faster than I thought.'
You feel a small, even pressure in your chest—less like panic than the tension of a pulled thread.
Your immediate reaction is practical: notes, calls, meetings.
But beneath that is a softer current, the thing
your hands already knew.
These renderings are not neutral—they rearrange the town's memory into sanitized vistas.
Where does the shell fit?
Where do the nets go?
You step forward and look at the renderings more closely.
The artist's hand has edited out laundry lines, hand-painted signs, the crooked lamppost by the lighthouse.
In the place of your mother's shell sits a plaza plaque, neat and descriptive.
A Saltworks Member Emergency hearing is scheduled for tomorrow night at Municipal Hall.
They're pitching it as stabilization.
(He shrugs.) 'They're calling for public input, but—'
Public input tends to be a checkbox in their versions.
(The sentence is firmer than you meant.
You look at Aunt Lila for a beat.
She watches you without saying anything, her expression unreadable in that way that carries so many things.)
We will go,' she says at last.
'We will say what we know.
We'll bring the shells, the nets, the songs.
You fold that into the small, rising compass of your day.
It's not a storm yet.
It's a line on a calendar, a rendering on a screen.
Still, something shifts beneath your practiced calm—an internal tightening
from muscle to spine.
The tide is a slow force; so too must be your response, but it is a force nonetheless.
You think of Elias Park's vane on your counter, of the Saltworks' hands in the sluice, of the way the seedlings bend toward light because someone planted them there.
What you want to protect is not
just structures but the accumulation of small habits and names—the way the town tells itself who it is.
Tomorrow night, then.
We'll patch, we'll prepare, we'll show up.
attend the hearing, bring evidence, rally voices, insist on alternatives that keep memory sewn into plans.
It feels exacting, a plan made of stitches and measurements rather than slogans.
• Draft talking points at the bench
community stories, legal protections, alternatives to wholesale displacement.
• Call Elias to tell him about the hearing
You press a call and let it ring.
When he answers, you talk without dramatics—just facts, the vane's note, a hearing, and whether he can bring prototypes.
His voice is steady on the line; you both fit plans around the other's breath.
The narrative continues with the outcome of the chosen action integrated into preparation for the hearing.
You choose to call Elias Park.
The call is a small, precise set of exchanges—enough to measure readiness but not to burden one another with certainty.
You sound like the tide—slow and sure.
What's up?
Whitlock's renderings are out.
Emergency hearing tonight.
(a short pause) 'Tonight?
That's sooner than—' (He exhales.) 'I can bring extra vanes.
If they try to frame it as 'only engineers can decide,' we'll show ponds, permeability, lived knowledge.
I can rig a couple of demos for the public plaza.'
We need something tangible.
People need to touch it and see that there are options.
(softly) 'I'll be there.
With prototypes and pictures.
And Mira—I'll keep the demo small.
Public, accessible.'
he'll come, he will bring his hands and his tools.
The connection is steady and unflashy—exactly like the tide.
You finish the call and fold the phone into your pocket.
Around you, the boardwalk resumes its low hum.
The sluice trickles on.
The seedlings shimmer.
The rendering on the kiosk scrolls to a new angle—a
wide plaza with children playing—but you now see the erased spots between the pixels where memory sits.
talk to Jun about demo placement; draft public questions; call the council clerk to confirm hearing format; assemble oral histories; bring Aunt Lila's shell.
Each bullet is a stitch.
not rallying cries, not breakdowns, but a compact set of actions that, woven together, might hold a town like a net holds fish—flexible, communal, and repairing.
You stand and fold the notebook closed.
The wind picks up, carrying salt and the faint scent of frying dough from a vendor.
You touch the coral scarf at your neck and feel its familiar weight, a small, human resistance to the tides of plan and power.
Outside, the sea breaths on the boards in long, indifferent exhalations.
Inside you, something practical and patient lines up to meet it.
You resolve to fight for a protection that keeps memory alive—not for spectacle, but for the small, stubborn stories that are the town's true resilience.
The day narrows to preparation and presence; tomorrow's meeting will be work and witness.
You feel the moment of decision as a quiet hinge.
You are not calling for revolution—only for the town to be allowed to remember itself as it changes.
Jun, oral histories, the clerk.
The list hums
under your skin the way tide lines hum on mud—maps of what must be done.
You think of Elias' voice agreeing to bring prototypes, of the steadiness in it that felt like a hand on the
back of a boat.
Noah is already there, kneeling by a pallet table, his paint-streaked hoodie damp with morning dew.
He waves without looking up, the motion catching a smear of sun across his freckled cheek.
Morning.
Coffee?
Or something stronger?
Coffee.
Black—no sugar.
And a steady hand with the mic, when the time comes.
Noah snorts softly.
He knows how you mean 'steady' and what it costs to be steady here.

Jun consults a tablet, thumbs moving with deliberate care.
Aunt Lila, wrapped in
layered cardigans, stands slightly apart, her eyes scanning the marsh like it is a ledger of names.
We can get the demo panels mounted by midday if we shift the volunteers.
Two teams—assembly and outreach.

People need to touch it.
Not just numbers—they need to feel it's possible.
Jun nods, but his eyes flick to Noah, then to the field of reeds beyond—where the town's memory is written in salt and marsh grass.
a
compact micro-grid node—tubed copper, a tiny turbine, and a panel of mismatched solar cells wired with care.
It looks less like a polished product and more like a living thing someone had coaxed together out of
scraps.
It's rough.
But it held an old light last night when the street went out.
I tightened the bearings.
Want to see?
Show me everything.
He smiles—small, private—and begins to explain, holding the device like an offering.
His hands are steady; his voice lowers so that the technicalities become a sort of intimacy.
The switch is here.
It isolates critical loads—lights, a pump, a comms node.
We could set up three of these across the boardwalk; they'd keep a few households and the clinic functional when the grid drops.
And who keeps them running?
Who owns them?
That's the long part.
I want them community-run—training, shared maintenance.
Low-cost tech, easy fixes.
No paperwork that pushes people out.
keep people in place, make systems that bend with the storm rather than bulldoze through what remains.
There's relief in the shape
of it, but it comes with a wet, metallic aftertaste—because relief here is a relative thing.
We can demo the node alongside Aunt Lila's oral history table.
People talk, kids play, they see a working light and remember that's what this is—living, not a monument.
Tell them the fisher's tale.
Tell them the lighthouse bell used to be rung by hands, not contracts.
Jun will handle the layout, Noah runs outreach, a group will collect oral histories.
You find yourself assigning yourself the legal groundwork—petition templates, questions for the council clerk—because the map in your head resists being abstract.
not bright, but insistently warm.

• Place the node where everyone can touch it
You slide the node to the front of the table, fingers tracing the soldered joints.
A few people lean forward, tentative hands reach out, and a child laughs when the tiny light blinks.
It is small and real and it anchors the conversation.

Elias looks at you, a question in his eyes.
'We'll explain,' you say.
The group looks at the covered kit as if it's a promise waiting to be opened.
It keeps the technical debate tidy—and distant.
immediacy or control.

straps cinched, placards painted, schedules clarified.

but private investors?

People pass, some glancing at your flyers, some not;

Tighter.

There is a softness at the edge of his voice—a patience that has always been there—and also a reserve.

I don't want you bearing that alone.

You ever think about how these tiny systems remind me of gardens?

I think about how the town remembers itself.
Aunt Lila says we are stitched to place by stories, and stories like the steady light.
That's a nicer engineering metaphor than I would write in a lab report.

The romantic edge is present but careful, folded into a shared task.
You both are guarded lovers of possibility rather than grand gestures.
If it helps—if the demo gets them to think of small systems as real, we could have more than sympathy.
We might get momentum.
Not immediate, but real.
Momentum is slower here than in ads.
But it lasts longer if it grows from people's hands.
Your chest tightens on the word 'longer' because every long thing here is frayed at the edges by storms and money.
You wonder if being slow is a virtue or a liability when someone glossy is selling a fast fix.
• Press Elias about his past failure
the funding shifted, timelines shortened, trust eroded.

It anchors your resolve.
• Keep the conversation focused on today's demo
You steer your questions back to logistics, to battery life and training modules.
Elias nods, grateful.
The past stays folded away; the present work proceeds with less risk of unraveling.
The choice is minor—flavor, a tug along the tightrope of personal and public—but it colors the next hour.
Whether you pry into old wounds or preserve the present focus changes the room's temperature by degrees.

The council chamber smells of lemon disinfectant and old paper.
Light from the bay slices through the glass in thin, cold bars; behind them, a glossy rendering of Cassandra 'Cass' Whitlock's redevelopment glows—clean lines, raised promenades,
landscaped seawalls that promise 'stability.' The projection washes the room in reassuring blues while the air feels thinner, like the gap between a promise and the shore.
You press your thumb into the coral scarf at your throat the same way you have since morning.
The fabric is a tether; the scarf is small, and yet it is all there is between you and the tide of polished words in front of you.

Her voice is clear, steady—solutions as bullet points, human costs in parentheses.

cost estimates, timeline projections, renderings of raised promenades framed by tidy trees.
